coredata.dc:description: Owing to the exorbitant CO2 activation energy and unsatisfactory photogenerated charge separation efficiency, CO2 photoconversion still faces enormous challenges. In this study, a directional electron transfer channel has been established by decorating N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) on the surface of Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles to ensure that more active electrons can participate in the CO2 reduction. The conduction band of Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles is calculated to be −1.55 eV versus the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE), pH = 7, which is negative enough to attain the photocatalytic CO2 reduction potential of −0.53 eV versus NHE, pH = 7. CO2 adsorption curves and in situ Fourier transform infrared spectra reveal that N-CQDs facilitate surface CO2 adsorption and activation, as well as CO desorption. In addition, steady-state photoluminescence and photoelectrochemical tests prove that the charge separation efficiency can be greatly enhanced by constructing N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composites. In the presence of pure water, N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 composite achieved a CO yield of 16.22 μmol g−1 after 5 h Xe light illumination, which was 3.24 times higher than that of pure Bi4MoO9 (4.98 μmol g−1). This study offers a distinctive approach to the optimization of Bi4MoO9 photocatalysts and their application in energy conversion.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the preparation process of the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composites; (b) TEM and (c) HRTEM images of N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 composite; (d) XRD patterns and (e) FT-IR spectra of N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composites and pure Bi4MoO9; (f) high-resolution XPS C 1 s spectrum of N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 composite; (g) DRS spectra of N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composites and Bi4MoO9.

(a) Curves of CO yield in photoreduction of CO2 over pure Bi4MoO9 and various N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composites under Xe lamp irradiation for 5 h in the presence of pure water (each set of photocatalytic activity experiments was repeated three times); (b) Stability tests for yield curves of CO2 photoreduction to CO over N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 composite under Xe lamp irradiation for 35 h in the presence of pure water (each set of photocatalytic activity experiments was repeated three times); (c) CO2 adsorption isotherms of pure Bi4MoO9 and N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 composite and (d) in situ FT-IR spectra of N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 composite.

(a) Steady-state PL spectra; (b) Transient photocurrent response curves measured in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7); (c) EIS curves of pure Bi4MoO9 and N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 composite in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/Fe(CN))6 4−; (d) Mott-Schottky plots obtained in 0.2 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution with frequencies of 500, 1000 and 1500 Hz; (e) XPS valence band spectrum; (f) (αEphoton)1/2 vs Ephoton curve of Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles.
Schematic diagram of a pathway for the possible separation and transfer of photogenerated charge carriers over N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 under light illumination.

head.ce:title: Dual modulation steering electron reducibility and transfer of bismuth molybdate nanoparticle to boost carbon dioxide photoreduction to carbon monoxide

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Road, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Road, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dual strategies on energy band modulation and surface charge utilization have been employed to fundamentally actualize the photocatalytic CO2 reduction performance of Bi4MoO9 materials. Herein, N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) decorated Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles was successfully synthesized, in which N-CQDs advanced the CO2 molecule adsorption capacity as well as the excited carriers’ separation efficiency. After 5h light irradiation, N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 composite exhibited more than 3 times higher CO yield rate than pure Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles.

Owing to the exorbitant CO2 activation energy and unsatisfactory photogenerated charge separation efficiency, CO2 photoconversion still faces enormous challenges. In this study, a directional electron transfer channel has been established by decorating N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) on the surface of Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles to ensure that more active electrons can participate in the CO2 reduction. The conduction band of Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles is calculated to be −1.55 eV versus the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE), pH = 7, which is negative enough to attain the photocatalytic CO2 reduction potential of −0.53 eV versus NHE, pH = 7. CO2 adsorption curves and in situ Fourier transform infrared spectra reveal that N-CQDs facilitate surface CO2 adsorption and activation, as well as CO desorption. In addition, steady-state photoluminescence and photoelectrochemical tests prove that the charge separation efficiency can be greatly enhanced by constructing N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composites. In the presence of pure water, N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 composite achieved a CO yield of 16.22 μmol g−1 after 5 h Xe light illumination, which was 3.24 times higher than that of pure Bi4MoO9 (4.98 μmol g−1). This study offers a distinctive approach to the optimization of Bi4MoO9 photocatalysts and their application in energy conversion.

ce:section.ce:para: Excessive CO2 emissions from the uncontrolled use of fossil fuels affect the global carbon circulation and aggravate a series of environmental problems.[1] The solar-light-driven conversion of CO2 into value-added carbon-based fuels is regarded as one of the most promising strategies for mitigating the greenhouse effect as well as supplying clean energy.[2–4] The CO2 molecule is extremely stable (ΔGθ f = 394.4 kJ/mol) owing to its linear symmetric structure. In addition, the extremely high dissociation energy of the CO bond (750 kJ/mol) implies that very high activation energy required,[5] and hence the conversion efficiency of photoreduction CO2 to value-added chemicals is still unsatisfactory. In addition, the complex multi-electron process for CO2 reduction retards the kinetics (CO2 + 2e− → CO; CO2 + 8e− → CH4; CO2 + 12e− → C2H4, etc.), which greatly limits the product selectivity.[6] Many advanced photocatalysts for photocatalytic CO2 reduction have been designed to increase product conversion and selectivity, such as metal oxides, metal halides, nonmetallic compounds and organic polymers.[7–10] Unfortunately, the photocatalytic CO2 conversion efficiency and selectivity of photocatalysts still restricted by their poor photogenerated charge separation and high activation potential.[11] Consequently, the construction of an efficient photocatalytic CO2 reduction system is imperative.

Bismuth molybdate, a typical Aurivillus-phase semiconductor material, has recently become a focus because of its unique crystal structure, tunable morphology and flexible band gap.[12] In particular, its crystal structure of alternating [Bi2O2] and octahedral [MoO] layers is conducive to the separation of electron-hole pairs, and can maintain a long diffusion length for the carriers.[13] Bismuth molybdate has diverse crystalline phases with the general chemical formula mBi2O3·nMoO3, including Bi2MoO6,[14] Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55,[15] Bi14MoO24,[16] and Bi4MoO9.[17] Current studies are mainly focused on Bi2MoO6 materials.[18–20] Lately, owing to a sufficiently positive valence band position, Bi4MoO9, which has a strong oxidation capacity has gained widespread attention for the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants.[13,17,21] Fang’s group reported the synthesis of a TiO2/Bi4MoO9 heterojunction via heterogeneous nucleation. The band gap and valence band maximum of Bi4MoO9 nanobelts were determined to be 2.70 and 2.94 eV, respectively. The extent of rhodamine B (RhB) degradation with the TiO2/Bi4MoO9 heterojunction reached nearly 100% after 45 min of visible-light irradiation.[13] Dong’s group synthesized a novel Bi0/Bi4MoO9 core/shell heterostructure photocatalyst with oxygen vacancies, which showed a maximum extent of nitric oxide removal of up to 55.4% under visible light irradiation, which was much higher than that of the original Bi4MoO9 microrods (12.7%). Moreover, the valence band position of Bi4MoO9 (3.48 eV) in this work is equal to the highest value reported in previous studies.[17] In addition, Yu’s group developed a zero-dimension/two-dimension Bi4MoO9 quantum dots/Bi2MoO6 nanosheets phase junction in order to accelerate efficient charge separation and migration. Under visible light irradiation for 180 min, the Bi4MoO9/Bi2MoO6 composite could completely degrade RhB.[21] The band gap and valence band maximum of Bi4MoO9 quantum dots were estimated to be 2.89 and 2.53 eV, respectively. It should be noted that the valence band position of the Bi4MoO9 quantum dots is distinctly upshifted in contrast to the previous two Bi4MoO9 materials in TiO2/Bi4MoO9 heterojunction and Bi4MoO9/Bi2MoO6 composite. Based on the above analysis, we assume that the band position of Bi4MoO9 could be flexibly adjusted by changing the Bi4MoO9 morphology, such as from nanobelts to spherical particles. However, although different band positions of Bi4MoO9 photocatalysts have been reported, their conduction band potential energy is still insufficient to attain the reduction potential of photocatalytic CO2 conversion (−0.53 eV versus the normal hydrogen electrode, NHE, pH = 7).[22] Hence, it is desirable to tailor the morphology of Bi4MoO9 material to further optimize its valence band and conduction band positions, thus appealing to the strict requirement of CO2 photoreduction.

In fact, the extremely low photogenerated charge utilization efficiency of intrinsic semiconductor materials is a nonnegligible issue in photocatalysis,[23] and as a result, the photocatalytic efficiency is still far from what is actually required. A variety of countermeasures have been developed to promote the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers, including semiconductor coupling[24] and defect engineering[25]. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a recently developed zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial with a size below 10 nm, can be used as a favorable electron acceptor to build surface separation centers.[26–29] Considering that the electron-deficient C atom in the CO2 molecule tends to combine with the electron-rich active sites on the photocatalysts,[30,31] CQDs with abundant electrons can serve as highly active sites to attract more CO2 molecules. In addition, CO2 molecules would preferentially adsorb on the CQDs surface and become further deactivated owing to the π–π interaction between the two.[32,33] Therefore, CQDs are candidates for constructing composite photocatalytic systems to boost the CO2 reduction activity of intrinsic photocatalysts. Many studies have shown that CQDs combined with different photocatalysts are effective in photocatalytic CO2 reduction.[34–36] MacFarlane’s group synthesized a novel CQDs/Cu2O heterostructure for solar-light-driven photocatalytic reduction of CO2 to methyl alcohol in pure water without any sacrificial reagents. After 4 h of light irradiation, the yield rate of the single product methyl alcohol was 213.6 µmol g−1, which is higher than that of pure Cu2O (34.4 µmol g−1). The improved electron transfer capability of the CQDs led to a remarkable increase in photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity.[37] Wang’s group used a mild one-step hydrothermal treatment to prepare g-C3N4 (CN), simultaneously introducing CQDs modification and oxygen atom doping (CQD/OCN).[34] The CQD/OCN-25% sample exhibited the best performance for visible light-driven photocatalytic CO2 reduction to CH4 (1.2 μmol g−1) after 8 h in the gas–solid system, which was about 14 times higher than that of CN. Importantly, the CH4 selectivity of all the composite samples was up to 70%, much higher than CN (11.1%). The above results verified that in situ modified CQDs would advance the photogenerated charge separation and migration of intrinsic semiconductors, as well as provide adsorption and activation sites for CO2 moleculars. Moreover, doping with nitrogen can effectively induce charge delocalization, which further expands the electron transfer ability of the CQDs.[38–42] Hence, a directional electron transport channel would be established by decorating N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) on the surface of Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles to enhance the efficient separation and utilization of photogenerated carriers, thus increasing the CO2 photoreduction performance.

In this work, Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles with a diameter of approximately 29 nm and with a conduction band potential fully feasible for CO2 reduction, were prepared via a simple solvothermal method. Subsequently, N-CQDs were introduced in situ onto the surface of Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles by electrostatic adsorption. The introduced N-CQDs realized higher charge separation efficiency and greatly increased electron density around the activity sites for CO2 adsorption. Impressively, after 5 h of light irradiation, the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 composite showed a maximum CO yield rate of 3.24 μmol g−1h−1 in pure water without any sacrificial reagents, which was 3.24 times higher than that of Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles (1.00 μmol g−1h−1). The mechanism of photocatalytic CO2 conversion over the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composite was revealed by in situ Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy. To the best of our knowledge, Bi4MoO9 photocatalysts have not been reported for CO2 photoreduction. This study revealed some distinctive insights into exploiting other semiconductor photocatalysts to advance the development of the conversion CO2 to value-added chemicals.

Bismuth nitrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), sodium molybdate (Na2MoO4·2H2O), ammonium citrate (C6H5O7(NH4)3), ethylenediamine, mannitol solution, nitric acid, sodium hydroxide, ethanol, ethylene glycol, potassium chloride (KCl) and sodium sulfate (Na2SO4) were analytically pure and were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.. 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (0.1 M PBS, pH = 7) was acquired from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Potassium ferrocyanide and potassium ferricyanide, both analytically pure and were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. The materials in this study were used directly without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Ammonium citrate (1.215 g) and ethylenediamine (0.335 mL) were successively dissolved in 10 mL of water. The mixed solution was poured into a Teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL), and the reaction temperature was maintained at 200 °C. The reaction was for 5 h, and the autoclave was then cooled to room temperature and the brown liquid obtained was dialyzed for 24 h to produce the required the required N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) solution.[40]

First, 0.2425 g Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was weighed precisely into a mixture of 5 mL of H2O and 5 mL of a 0.1 mol L–1 (M) mannitol solution, and then 1 mL nitric acid was added with constant stirring. A 0.1513 g portion of Na2MoO4·2H2O was dissolved in the above solution, and the pH was adjusted to 12 using 1 M sodium hydroxide with constant stirring. After stirring for 30 min, the solution was transferred to a 50 mL autoclave and heated in a drying oven at 160 °C for 3 h. The product was collected by centrifugation, washed three times with deionized water and absolute alcohol, and dried at 60 °C overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-doped carbon quantum dots/bismuth molybdate composites
ce:section.ce:para: First, the Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles were dispersed in 20 mL ethylene glycol, and precisely measured 1, 3 and 5 mL portions of the prepared N-CQDs solution were added to the nanoparticle dispersion, respectively, followed by ultrasonic treatment for 2 h. Finally, the products were washed several times with deionized water and absolute alcohol, respectively, and dried at 60 °C overnight. The resulting products were named N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-1, N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2, and N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-3, respectively.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and corresponding high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) micrographs were obtained using an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN Field Emission Electron Microscope. The crystal structures of the Bi4MoO9 and N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composites were confirmed using a Shimadzu XRD-6000 diffractometer (XRD) with a Cu-Kα radiation source. Raman spectra were obtained using laser Raman spectrometer (Renishaw Invia). A Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (Nexus 470 FT-IR) was used to determine the functional groups using the KBr disk technique. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a PHI5300 instrument with a monochromatic Mg Kα source. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were recorded using a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrometer. The BET (Brunauer-Emmett-Teller, Micromeritics In-strument Corporarion, USA) method was employed to characterize the specific surface area of the Bi4MoO9 and N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composites using the N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms, and CO2 adsorption isotherms of the samples were also obtained (Micromeritics Instrument Corporation, USA). In situ FT-IR spectra were acquired using a Thermo Fisher Nicolet iZ10 (USA). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the as-prepared samples were recorded using a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrometer. The photoelectrochemical performance was recorded on a CHI660B electrochemical system (Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd.) with a Pt wire as the counter electrode, an Ag/AgCl/sat. electrode as the reference electrode, and a thin film of the as-prepared materials on indium tin oxide conductive glass as the working electrode. The photocurrent, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, and Mott-Schottky plots were performed in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7), 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/Fe(CN))6 4−, and 0.2 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution, at frequencies of 500, 1000, and 1500 Hz, respectively.

The photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity of the as-prepared Bi4MoO9 and N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composites was evaluated using a Labsolar-6A online gas system (Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co., Ltd.). Bi4MoO9 (30 mg) or one of its composite materials was dispersed in 50 mL of H2O. First, the glass reactor was evacuated to 0 kPa, and high-purity CO2 gas (99.99%) was injected into the reactor at 88 kPa. The photocatalytic reduction reaction was maintained at 5 °C by circulating cooling water. The light source was a 300 W Xe lamp (PLS-SXE 300D (BF), Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co., Ltd.). Gas chromatography was employed analyze the possible generated products CO, CH4, C2H4, C2H6, etc. using a FULI GC9790II (China) gas chromatograph equipped with an FID detector.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image in Figure S1
a revealed that the particle-like morphology of the Bi4MoO9 material nanoparticles with diameters of approximately 29 nm had been successfully synthesized. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) indicated a lattice spacing measured to be 0.327 nm ( Figure S1
b), which matched well with the (111) crystal planes of the Bi4MoO9 material.[13] Notably, after the introduction of N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), the N-CQDs were evenly distributed on the surface of the Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles, while the nanoparticle morphology of the Bi4MoO9 material was still maintained ( Figure 1
b, S1c, and S1d). Lattice fringes with lattice spacings of 0.322 and 0.327 nm were carefully measured in the enlarged regions ( Figure 1
c), and these belong to the (002) planes of N-CQDs and the (111) planes of Bi4MoO9, respectively.[13,43] It was preliminarily proved that N-CQDs were successfully decorated on the Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles to form a composite system.

To determine the effect on the crystalline phase structure of Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles of introducing N-CQDs via electrostatic interaction, the as-prepared Bi4MoO9 and N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 samples with different N-CQDs content were examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD). The results in Figure 1
d show that all distinctive peaks can be well indexed to the face-centered cubic phase of Bi4MoO9 materials (JCPDS card No. 12–0149).[13,17,21] No characteristic peaks attributed to N-CQDs were observed in the XRD patterns of the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composites, which is probably due to the low load and high dispersion of N-CQDs.[41] In addition, the D and G bands respectively centered at 1357 and 1545 cm−1 assigned to the carbon materials did not appear in the Raman spectra of the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composites, although they might be concealed by the strong background absorption peak originating from the N-CQDs fluorescence effect ( Figure S2 ).[40,44] However, two characteristic bands were observed at 305 and 880 cm−1 in the Raman spectra of pristine Bi4MoO9 and the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composites, and are attributed to Mo–O stretching and O–Mo–O bending modes of [MoO4] tetrahedral species.[21] As the N-CQDs content increased, these two peaks weakened, suggesting a change in the surface chemical environment of the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composites. This phenomenon might be caused by interaction between the N-CQDs and Bi4MoO9 in the close contact.[45] Additionally, no Raman band shift or other new characteristic peaks were observed in the Raman spectra of the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composites, confirming that introduction of N-CQDs did not change the intrinsic structure of the Bi4MoO9 material.

The surface functional groups of the as-prepared photocatalysts were identified by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, further confirming the presence of N-CQDs in the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composites. As can be seen in Figure 1
e, several new absorption peaks occurred in the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 materials compared to pure Bi4MoO9. The characteristic peaks at 1375 and 1610 cm−1 were assigned to the –NH bending vibration and the CC stretching vibration, respectively.[46,47] In addition, three absorption peaks at 1027, 1080, and 1699 cm−1 were attributed to CO, CNHC, and CN, respectively.[48–50] The characteristic absorption peak at 504 cm−1 corresponds to the Bi–O stretching mode.[51] The results of FT-IR characterization proved that the N-CQDs successfully coalesced with Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles.

To investigate the elemental composition and surface chemical state of the synthesized materials, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out, and the results are shown in Figures S3 and S4 . The survey spectrum of N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 revealed the presence of the elements C, N, Bi, O, and Mo ( Figure S3 ), further verifying that N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composites were successfully synthesized via ultrasonic treatment. High-resolution XPS spectra of Bi, O, and Mo elements were collected and are shown in Figure S4
a-c. The binding energies at 164.2 and 158.9 eV of the Bi4MoO9 material were attributed to Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2, respectively, indicating that the valence state of the Bi is stable Bi3+ . [52] In contrast to the Bi4MoO9 material, the Bi 4f characteristic peaks of the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 composite displayed a shift towards lower binding energy, which might be attributed to the strong mutual interaction between N-CQDs and Bi4MoO9 changing the chemical environment of Bi. Similarly, the characteristic peaks displayed in the O 1 s and Mo 3d high-resolution XPS spectra of the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 composite material also shifted towards lower binding energy compared to the monomer. The peak at 530.3 eV in Figure S4
b was assigned to the lattice oxygen in the Bi4MoO9 material. In the Mo 3d high-resolution XPS spectra ( Figure S4
c), the two peaks at 234.8 and 231.7 eV correspond to Mo 3d3/2 and Mo 3d5/2, respectively, indicating that the Mo in N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 exists as Mo6+.[11] At the same time, the C 1 s peaks at 284.6, 285.6, and 287.8 eV belong to the CC/CC bond, oxygen-linked carbon, and nitrogen-linked carbon atoms, respectively ( Figure 1
f).[53,54] As shown in Figure S4
d, the peak located at 397.6 eV in the XPS N 1 s high-resolution spectrum corresponds to the NH bond in the N-CQDs.[55] The slight displacement of these characteristic peaks indicates that the superficial chemical environment of the Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles changed as a result of the strong interaction between Bi4MoO9 and N-CQDs. All the XPS results are consistent with the Raman and FT-IR spectra. To fully investigate the effect on the optical properties of the as-synthesized N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composites and Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles, the light absorption band edge was further obtained by UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) and the corresponding results are shown in Figure 1
g. The absorption band edge of the Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles was determined to be 450 nm. With an increase in the N-CQDs content, no apparent variation in the absorption band edge can be observed. The band tails in the range 450–800 nm of the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composites exhibited slightly enhanced light absorption compared to pure Bi4MoO9 due to the up-conversion property of N-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance of carbon dioxide reduction
ce:section.ce:para:
To ascertain the function of the N-CQDs, the performance of photocatalytic CO2 conversion over the samples was evaluated. Photocatalytic CO2 reduction experiments were performed under light irradiation (300 W Xe lamp) in pure water without any sacrificial agent, co-catalyst, or dditional photosensitizer. As shown in Figure 2
a, the sole product detected during the entire photocatalytic CO2 reduction process was CO. The photocatalytic experiment results clearly indicated that the introduction of N-CQDs increased the rate of CO2 conversion to CO. After light irradiation for 5 h, N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 composite showed the highest photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity with a CO yield rate of 3.24 μmol g−1h−1, which was more than three times higher than that of pure Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles (1.00 μmol g−1h−1). The photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity of other reported photocatalyst systems under the comparable conditions was shown in Table S1. Moreover, the long-term stability of the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 composite activity was also tracked, and the results are shown in Figure 2
b. It can be observed that the total CO yield was 89.87 μmol g−1 when the photocatalytic reaction time was prolonged to 35 h. The photocatalytic activity after prolonged reaction did not decrease drastically, indicating good photostability and activity stability of N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2. Moreover, the XRD pattern and TEM images of the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 composite after the CO2 photoreduction reaction showed no apparent changes ( Figures S6-S9 ). The elemental composition and surface chemical states of the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 composite after illumination for 35 h were analyzed by XPS, and the results are displayed in Figure S10 . It can be seen that no new characteristic peaks appeared in the XPS spectra of the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 composite after prolonged reaction, and the shifts of all binding energies were also stable over this time, further validating that N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 retained high structural stability during the process of photocatalytic CO2 reduction.[57–59] Furthermore, in the control experiments whose results are shown in Figure S11 , no CO was detected in the dark, or when an argon atmosphere replaced CO2, or in the absence of the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 photocatalyst, indicating that CO was indeed generated from photocatalytic CO2 conversion over the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of photocatalytic carbon dioxide reduction
ce:section.ce:para:
To gain deeper insight into the CO2 photoreduction process over N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composites, the adsorption and activation properties of CO2 were studied. The CO2 adsorption isotherms in Figure 2
c proved that the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 composite exhibited a stronger CO2 adsorption ability than Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles owing to the π-π interaction between the N-CQDs and CO2 molecules.[60] The characteristic peaks at 1362, 1396, 1418, 1507 and 1534 cm−1 in the in situ FT-IR spectra depicted in Figure 2
d are ascribed to monodentate carbonate (m-CO3 2−); the peaks of bidentate carbonate (b-CO3 2−) are identified at 1642 and 1667 cm−1, and the absorption peaks at 1453 and 1492 cm−1 are attributed to bicarbonate (HCO3 −). Furthermore, the peak located at 1554 cm−1 is ascribed to the *COOH group, which is one of the key intermediates in the photoreduction of CO2 to CO.[11,61–63] Notably, the absorption peaks increased in intensity with increasing illumination time, implying that CO2 molecules were constantly adsorbed and converted into active carbon species during the CO2 photoreduction process. Contact angle measurements are commonly used to analyze the surface characteristics of semiconductor photocatalysts, and as shown in Figure S10 , the contact angle measured for the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composite was 17°, which is smaller than that of pure Bi4MoO9 (21°). This means that the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composite material has higher surface hydrophilicity, benefiting the adsorption of H2O on the surface to ensure the proton supply in the CO2 reduction reaction.[64]

The behavior of photogenerated charge carriers in N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composites was explored to clarify the role of N-CQDs in electron transfer. Figure 3
a shows the steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectra of pure Bi4MoO9 and N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2. The N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 composite showed lower PL intensity, indicating that the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 composite has a lower photogenerated carrier recombination rate compared to Bi4MoO9 materials. Moreover, a higher photocurrent response of semiconductor photocatalysts under light irradiation implies that more electrons can be generated to participate in the CO2 reduction reaction. As shown in Figure 3
b, the N-CQDs-modified Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles have a higher photocurrent response than pure Bi4MoO9 materials, thus benefitting from the strong electron conduction ability of the N-CQDs. Generally, the arc radius in the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) indicates resistance on the electrode surface. Therefore, the smaller the radius of the Nyquist arc, the lower the impedance to photogenerated carriers in the migration process, and the faster transfer of interface charges.[65] Therefore, the smaller Nyquist arc radius of the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 sample indicates that the resistance to charge carrier transfer in the as-prepared composite is much lower ( Figure 3
c). The above characterization results verify that the separated photogenerated charges from the bulk phase of Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles migrated to the interface of the N-CQDs via efficient interfacial contact, which is consistent with the experimental results of photocatalytic CO2 reduction.[66,67]

The band edge position of a semiconductor is particularly important for deriving the photocatalytic mechanism, which determines the thermodynamic possibility of a photocatalytic reaction. In the Mott-Schottky (MS) diagram ( Figure 3
d), the slope of the MS curve is positive in the linear region, demonstrating that the Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles were n-type semiconductors. The intercept on the x (voltage) axis of the MS curve shows that the flat-band potential was −0.43 V versus normal hydrogen electrode (vs. NHE), pH = 7. The gap between the Fermi level and valence band (VB) of the Bi4MoO9 material was 1.14 eV measured by XPS valence band spectroscopy ( Figure 3
e).[68] Since the flat-band potential of an n-type semiconductor approximates to the Fermi level, it can be calculated that the valence band energy (EVB) of the Bi4MoO9 material is 0.91 eV (vs NHE, pH = 7). According to the Tauc plot in Figure 3
f, the band gap (Eg) of Bi4MoO9 materials was determined to be 2.46 eV. Based on the equation ECB = EVB − Eg, the conduction band energy (ECB) of the Bi4MoO9 materials was estimated to be −1.55 eV (vs. NHE, pH = 7), which is the most negative among reported studies.[69] The results demonstrate that the band position can be effectively adjusted by controlling the morphology of the Bi4MoO9 material. Based on the above analysis of the energy bands for pure Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles, a possible photocatalytic CO2 reduction mechanism is proposed, as shown in Figure 4
. In the pure Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles, the sample was excited by light irradiation, and photogenerated charge carriers were generated immediately. Some of the charge carriers that were transferred to the surface of the Bi4MoO9 material would participate in the subsequent CO2 molecule activation and CO dissociation. However, the great majority of the generated charges would recombine during migration, which restricts the photocatalytic CO2 conversion efficiency. For N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composites, the separated electrons could quickly transfer to the N-CQDs surface via benign interfacial contact, thus, a high photogenerated carrier separation efficiency would be actualized. In addition, owing to the π-π interaction between the CQDs and the CO2 molecule, N-CQDs with active electrons were more susceptible to the CO2 molecule adsorption and activation. Therefore, the introduced N-CQDs increased the utilization of photogenerated electrons and the CO2 adsorption, thus significantly boosting the CO yield of the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composite system.

Morphology tailoring is an effective way to tune the band structure of photocatalysts. Hence, in this work, Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles with 29 nm diameters were initially synthesized via solvothermal treatment. The conduction band position of the Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles attained the CO2 reduction potential, and was equal to the most negative among the previously reported studies.[12,16,20] Based on this, a directional charge transfer channel was established by constructing N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)/Bi4MoO9 composite system to radically overcome the sluggish charge separation and high CO2 activation energy barrier in the intrinsic Bi4MoO9. Compared to pure Bi4MoO9 nanoparticles, the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9 composites exhibited a clearly improved photocatalytic CO2 conversion efficiency, with the N-CQDs/Bi4MoO9-2 composite achieving a yield rate in CO evolution more than 3.24 times higher than that of the Bi4MoO9 materia. A series of characterization results demonstrated that the efficient interfacial contact between N-CQDs and Bi4MoO9 promotes the separation of charge carriers enables more dynamic electrons to be transferred to N-CQDs and participate in successful CO2 activation, thus greatly boosting the photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity. This work provides a unique standpoint for the rational design of Bi-based photocatalysts to open up their potential applications in CO2 resource utilization.
